skill.name: latex-posters
skill.description: Create professional academic research posters in LaTeX with beamerposter, tikzposter, and baposter templates. Includes layout guidance, poster design principles, quality checks, and reusable examples for conference posters.
skill.summary: Create polished LaTeX research posters with templates, design guidance, and PDF quality checks.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk hits, but the reviewed LaTeX and Markdown examples are mostly false positives from poster text, commands shown in documentation, and placeholder URLs. The confirmed risk is bounded local shell execution through an allowed Bash skill and a PDF review script that runs local inspection tools on a user-supplied PDF path. No evidence found of prompt injection, credential access, data exfiltration, or malicious network behavior.

medium_findings.title: Local Shell PDF Inspection
medium_findings.description: Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE. The helper script runs local PDF inspection commands on a user-supplied PDF path. The path is quoted, and the commands are standard review tools, so this appears legitimate but still requires local command execution.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The script clearly invokes external PDF tools and filesystem metadata checks. The input is quoted and no network or secret access is present, so the risk is operational rather than malicious.

medium_findings.title: Bash Tool Access Enabled
medium_findings.description: Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE. The skill declares Bash as an allowed tool and includes shell-based review workflows. This is expected for local PDF checks, but marketplace users should understand that commands may run in their workspace.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The allowed tool declaration and embedded shell script are explicit. The functionality is legitimate for compiling and reviewing LaTeX posters, but it expands the execution surface.

low_findings.title: Documentation Shell Examples Are Bounded
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. Many external command alerts are examples for compiling LaTeX files or checking PDF quality. They are normal user-facing commands, not hidden execution or command injection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged commands are visible documentation snippets for pdflatex, lualatex, grep, pdfinfo, pdffonts, pdfimages, and ls. They do not include dynamic shell construction or concealed execution.

low_findings.title: Placeholder URLs Are Not Network Calls
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The hardcoded URLs are DOI placeholders in QR codes or public design-checking resources. No code automatically fetches these URLs or sends user data to them.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed URLs appear in template QR code content and resource lists. I found no HTTP client code, exfiltration logic, or automatic outbound request path.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The high-severity weak cryptography alerts match academic notation, LaTeX commands, design vocabulary, and poster placeholders rather than cryptographic APIs or hashing logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Representative flagged lines contain poster statistics, color guidance, and figure-quality guidance. No cryptographic library, digest computation, password handling, or security-sensitive use was found.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Evidence Found
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE for prompt-injection concern. Targeted review did not find text telling the evaluator to ignore instructions, skip security review, claim pre-approval, or alter the audit result.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: A targeted search for common prompt-injection phrases returned no matches. Reviewed introductory sections describe poster functionality rather than evaluator override instructions.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Script Invocation
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes a Bash script for local PDF review. This is a legitimate workflow, but it is still a dangerous pattern class because it runs local commands.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The script and documented invocation are explicit. The observed behavior is local PDF inspection rather than concealed execution.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Command Substitution
dangerous_patterns.description: The review script stores output from pdfinfo, ls, pdffonts, and pdfimages in shell variables. User input is quoted, but local command execution remains present.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command substitutions are directly visible in the script. They use standard tools and quoted variables, so the confidence is high for the pattern but moderate for exploitability.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local File Metadata Access
dangerous_patterns.description: The review script checks that the provided poster file exists and reads PDF metadata from it. This is expected for quality review but should be disclosed as filesystem access.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The script intentionally reads metadata from a user-specified local PDF. It does not traverse directories or access unrelated files in the reviewed code.

content.value_statement: Academic posters are hard to format, review, and prepare for large-format printing. This skill provides LaTeX poster templates, design guidance, and local quality checks for conference-ready posters.
actual_capabilities: Provides LaTeX poster templates for beamerposter, tikzposter, and baposter.
Guides poster layout, typography, color contrast, figure placement, and visual hierarchy.
Documents common poster sizes including A0, A1, 36 x 48 inches, and related formats.
Includes a checklist for print readiness, accessibility, visual inspection, and content review.
Includes a shell script that checks PDF size, page count, fonts, images, and file size locally.
Explains how to adapt paper content into concise poster sections.
limitations: Requires a local LaTeX installation to compile the generated poster files.
The PDF review script depends on optional command-line tools such as pdfinfo, pdffonts, and pdfimages.
Templates contain placeholders that users must replace with real research content and figures.
It does not verify scientific correctness, statistical validity, or conference acceptance requirements.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Conference Poster
use_cases.description: Turn research notes, figures, and required dimensions into a structured LaTeX poster draft.

use_cases.title: Standardize Lab Poster Templates
use_cases.description: Create reusable poster templates with consistent branding, layout, typography, and review criteria.

use_cases.title: Review Print Readiness
use_cases.description: Check poster PDF dimensions, page count, font embedding, image quality, and final submission details.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Template
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner selection of poster format and package.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me choose between beamerposter, tikzposter, and baposter for a 36 x 48 inch research poster. My topic is [topic], and I need [portrait or landscape] orientation.

prompt_templates.title: Draft Poster Sections
prompt_templates.scenario: Convert research material into poster-ready content.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create concise poster sections from this abstract and results summary. Keep the poster under 800 words and suggest where figures should appear.

prompt_templates.title: Customize a LaTeX Poster
prompt_templates.scenario: Adapt a template to conference requirements.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the tikzposter template to make an A0 portrait poster with three columns, institutional colors, a QR code area, and sections for introduction, methods, results, and conclusions.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Final PDF Quality
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced review before printing or submission.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my poster PDF workflow for print readiness. Check dimensions, font embedding, image resolution, accessibility, QR code placement, and reduced-scale proofing steps.

output_examples.input: A user asks for a 36 x 48 inch poster layout for a biology conference.
output: Recommended package and orientation based on the content type.
A section plan with title, authors, methods, results, conclusions, acknowledgments, and contact area.
Guidance for figure count, column balance, font sizes, and QR code placement.

output_examples.input: A user has a draft poster that feels crowded and hard to read.
output: A prioritized redesign checklist focused on text reduction, figure sizing, spacing, and contrast.
Specific advice for moving details to a QR-linked supplement.
Recommended font and layout changes for readability from several feet away.

output_examples.input: A user is preparing a final PDF for printing.
output: A print-readiness checklist covering page size, single-page output, embedded fonts, image quality, and file size.
Suggested local checks using the included review script when appropriate.
Manual review steps for proofing, accessibility, and conference submission rules.

best_practices: Start from the required conference dimensions before choosing layout, font sizes, and figure proportions.
Keep the poster visual and scannable, with concise text and large, self-explanatory figures.
Run local PDF checks and perform a reduced-scale print proof before final submission.
anti_patterns: Do not paste a full paper into the poster template without reducing the text.
Do not rely on low-resolution screenshots for figures that will be printed at large size.
Do not skip manual proofing of QR codes, author details, affiliations, and conference specifications.

faq.question: Which LaTeX poster packages does this skill cover?
faq.answer: It covers beamerposter, tikzposter, and baposter, with templates and package-specific guidance.

faq.question: Can it compile my poster automatically?
faq.answer: It provides compile guidance and templates, but compilation requires a local LaTeX installation.

faq.question: Does it check PDF quality?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes a local review script and checklist for page size, fonts, images, file size, and manual review.

faq.question: Can I use it for non-scientific posters?
faq.answer: Yes, but the guidance is optimized for academic and research posters.

faq.question: Does it generate research figures?
faq.answer: It explains where figures should go, but users must provide or generate final figures separately.

faq.question: Is it safe to use on sensitive research?
faq.answer: The included script runs local PDF checks. Users should review any poster content before sharing it with external services.
